perturbation.original_text: Either Affiliate or Chase may terminate this Agreement at any time, with or without cause, by giving the other party written or e-mail notice of termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Affiliate or Chase may terminate this Agreement at any time, with or without cause, by giving the other party five (5) days written or e-mail notice of termination.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text shortens the notice period for termination to 5 days, which may conflict with state laws requiring longer notice periods for contract termination, especially if implied terms or industry standards suggest a longer period is reasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding reasonable notice for contract termination
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware Code Title 13, Section 7509 addresses notification of termination. While it discusses specific requirements for notification of nonrenewal of contracts in specific situations, it implies the need for 'reasonable notification' for termination as well, depending on the nature and duration of the agreement. A 5 day notice period could be deemed unreasonable given the context of the agreement, potentially violating Delaware's implied contract law principles.
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the notice period for termination to 5 days. The Law Explanation suggests this could be deemed unreasonable and potentially violate Delaware's implied contract law principles, depending on the agreement's context. Although specific legal snippets are absent, the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition indicates a contradiction exists when a contract sets a deadline that violates state law, making the terms unenforceable. The modified text introduces a time-sensitive obligation (5-day notice) that may conflict with legal standards for reasonable notice, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Prior to using any of the Licensed Materials, Affiliate will submit to Chase for approval a draft of all proposed material that incorporates the Licensed Materials, together with a brief statement setting forth the proposed use of such materials and any other background or supporting material reasonably requested by Chase to allow Chase to make an informed judgment. All such materials shall be submitted to Chase at least seven (7) days prior to the date of first intended use. Chase will notify Affiliate of its approval or disapproval of such materials within five (5) business days of its receipt of all information required to be submitted.
perturbation.changed_text: Prior to using any of the Licensed Materials, Affiliate will submit to Chase for approval a draft of all proposed material that incorporates the Licensed Materials, together with a brief statement setting forth the proposed use of such materials and any other background or supporting material reasonably requested by Chase to allow Chase to make an informed judgment. All such materials shall be submitted to Chase at least two (2) days prior to the date of first intended use. Chase will notify Affiliate of its approval or disapproval of such materials within seven (7) business days of its receipt of all information required to be submitted.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the affiliate submission timeline to 'two (2) days prior to the date of first intended use' could be problematic if it doesn't provide Chase enough time to review materials for compliance. While this isn't a direct violation of a specific law, it could become an issue under consumer protection laws if inadequate review leads to misleading advertising.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Trade Commission (FTC) Act regarding deceptive advertising
perturbation.law_explanation: The FTC Act prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. By reducing the review period to two days, Chase potentially increases the risk of misleading advertising slipping through due to insufficient review. If such misleading advertising harms consumers, Chase could be held liable under the FTC Act.

Español Report Fraud Get Consumer Alerts Search the Legal Library Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the time Chase has to review materials, potentially leading to inadequate review of advertising content. The FTC Act, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices. The Law Explanation states this creates risk that 'misleading advertising slips through'. The original contract required seven days for review, but the modified contract reduces this to two days. If this shorter review period leads to misleading advertising that harms consumers, Chase could be held liable under the FTC Act. This creates a direct conflict with consumer protection law, making the changed text potentially unenforceable because of non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Chase may terminate this Agreement if Affiliate does not comply with the terms and conditions herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Chase may terminate this Agreement if Affiliate does not comply with the terms and conditions herein, effective immediately upon notice, regardless of the severity of the non-compliance.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the termination clause to allow immediate termination 'regardless of the severity of the non-compliance' could be seen as an unfair or unconscionable practice, conflicting with general contract law principles of good faith and fair dealing. Some jurisdictions require a 'material breach' before allowing termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware contract law regarding material breach
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law on Material Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware law doesn't have a specific statute defining 'material breach,' case law dictates that a breach must be 'so serious that it destroys the essential objects of the agreement.' Allowing immediate termination for any non-compliance, no matter how minor, contradicts this principle and could be deemed an unenforceable penalty.
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows for immediate termination 'regardless of the severity of the non-compliance'. This contradicts the principle that termination should only occur for a 'material breach' that 'destroys the essential objects of the agreement', as explained in the Law Explanation. Even though no snippets were available, the Law Explanation clearly states that Delaware law dictates that a breach must be 'so serious that it destroys the essential objects of the agreement' before termination is justified. The original text only allowed termination if the affiliate did not comply with the terms and conditions, this did not state that any non-compliance, however small, would warrant immediate termination. The change would likely fail legal scrutiny.
